```{r}
    `95th pct`  = dollar(round(quantile(sim_flood[sim_flood > 0], 0.95))),
    `99th pct`  = dollar(round(quantile(sim_flood[sim_flood > 0], 0.99)))
  ),
  tibble(
    Hazard      = "Earthquake ($)",
    Basis       = "Conditional on occurrence",
    `Pr(> 0)`   = round(mean(sim_eq > 0), 4),
    `Mean`      = dollar(round(mean(sim_eq[sim_eq > 0]))),
    `50th pct`  = dollar(round(quantile(sim_eq[sim_eq > 0], 0.50))),
    `95th pct`  = dollar(round(quantile(sim_eq[sim_eq > 0], 0.95))),
    `99th pct`  = dollar(round(quantile(sim_eq[sim_eq > 0], 0.99)))
  )
)

# Combined table
bind_rows(uncond, cond) %>%
  arrange(Hazard, Basis) %>%
  kable(
    caption = paste0(
      "Unconditional vs. conditional marginal statistics — ",
      "N = 100,000 simulated years. Unconditional includes all years ",
      "regardless of whether a loss occurred. Conditional restricts to ",
      "years with positive loss only."
    )
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  collapse_rows(columns = 1, valign = "middle")
```

### Aggregate Annual Loss

Having simulated each of the above variables, we use them to construct
an updated expectual annual loss. Notice each simulated variable
captures different components of FEMA's
$\text{EAL} = \text{Exposure} \times AF \times HLR$ formula.

1.  **sim_tornado** - only replaces the AF part for Tornado (measured
    in count);
2.  **sim_flood** - The lognormal distribution handles the
    $HLR\times Exposure$ part for flood hazard for building loss only
    (measured in dollar amount) and Bernoulli distribution handles the
    AF part;
3.  **sim_eq** - same as flood.

Population and agriculture loss components for flood and earthquake,
as well as the full EAL for all non-simulated hazards, are held at
their reconstructed point estimates and added as fixed constants to
every simulated year.

```{r}
#| label: agg-loss

# Tornado: simulated COUNT × fixed (Exposure × HLR)
tornado_dmg_data <- sheldus_county_clean %>%
  filter(Hazard == "Tornado") %>%
  summarise(mean_dmg  = mean(prop_dmg_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
            n_records = n())

cat("Tornado records used for mean damage:", tornado_dmg_data$n_records, "\n")
cat("Fixed mean damage per event (Exposure × HLR per occurrence):",
    dollar(round(tornado_dmg_data$mean_dmg)), "\n")

mean_dmg_per_tornado <- ifelse(
  tornado_dmg_data$n_records > 0,
  tornado_dmg_data$mean_dmg,
  5e6  # if there is no tornado data, use $5M damage as backup
)

tornado_loss <- sim_tornado * mean_dmg_per_tornado

# Flood and earthquake: simulated variable already account for all components of the building loss

# Fixed population & agriculture point estimates, per hazard so sa_run() can use them
eal_tornado_pop_agr <- eal_by_hc %>%
  filter(nri_prefix == "TRND",
         consequence %in% c("Population", "Agriculture")) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(EAL_hc, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(total)

eal_flood_pop_agr <- eal_by_hc %>%
  filter(nri_prefix %in% c("RFLD", "CFLD"),
         consequence %in% c("Population", "Agriculture")) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(EAL_hc, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(total)

eal_eq_pop_agr <- eal_by_hc %>%
  filter(nri_prefix == "ERQK",
         consequence %in% c("Population", "Agriculture")) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(EAL_hc, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(total)

# Reconstructed NRI reference — all consequences, 3 simulated hazards only
# This is the single-point benchmark the MC distribution is compared against
eal_recon_3haz_full <- eal_by_hc %>%
  filter(nri_prefix %in% c("TRND", "RFLD", "CFLD", "ERQK")) %>%
  summarise(eal = sum(EAL_hc, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(eal)

# Full EAL for all non-simulated hazards (unchanged)
eal_fixed_other_hazards <- eal_by_hc %>%
  filter(!nri_prefix %in% c("RFLD", "CFLD", "ERQK", "TRND")) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(EAL_hc, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(total)

eal_fixed_total <- eal_tornado_pop_agr +
                   eal_flood_pop_agr +
                   eal_eq_pop_agr +
                   eal_fixed_other_hazards

cat("Fixed point estimate breakdown:\n")
cat("  Tornado pop + agr EAL:             ", dollar(round(eal_tornado_pop_agr)), "\n")
cat("  Flood pop + agr EAL:               ", dollar(round(eal_flood_pop_agr)), "\n")
cat("  Earthquake pop + agr EAL:          ", dollar(round(eal_eq_pop_agr)), "\n")
cat("  Full EAL for non-simulated hazards:", dollar(round(eal_fixed_other_hazards)), "\n")
cat("  Total fixed addition per year:     ", dollar(round(eal_fixed_total)), "\n")
cat("  Reconstructed NRI (3 hazards, all consequences):",
    dollar(round(eal_recon_3haz_full)), "\n")

agg_loss <- tornado_loss + sim_flood + sim_eq + eal_fixed_total

#  skewness and kurtosis
agg_mean   <- mean(agg_loss)
agg_sd     <- sd(agg_loss)
skewness_m <- (sum((agg_loss - agg_mean)^3) / N_sim) / agg_sd^3

tibble(
  Statistic = c("Mean ($)", "Median ($)", "SD ($)","95th percentile ($)", "99th percentile ($)","Skewness", "Kurtosis (excess)"),
  Value     = c(dollar(round(mean(agg_loss))),
                dollar(round(median(agg_loss))),
                dollar(round(sd(agg_loss))),
                dollar(round(quantile(agg_loss, 0.95))),
                dollar(round(quantile(agg_loss, 0.99))),
                round(skewness_m, 3),
                round((sum((agg_loss - agg_mean)^4) / N_sim) / agg_sd^4 - 3, 2))
) %>%
  kable(caption = paste0(
  "Summary of Aggregate annual loss")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r}
# If we just want to see the simulated part without fixed part
total_sim_loss <- tornado_loss + sim_flood + sim_eq

#  skewness and kurtosis
total_sim_mean   <- mean(total_sim_loss)
total_sim_sd     <- sd(total_sim_loss)
total_sim_skew <- (sum((total_sim_loss - total_sim_mean)^3) / N_sim) / total_sim_sd^3

tibble(
  Statistic = c("Mean ($)", "Median ($)", "SD ($)","95th percentile ($)", "99th percentile ($)","Skewness", "Kurtosis (excess)"),
  Value     = c(dollar(round(mean(total_sim_loss))),
                dollar(round(median(total_sim_loss))),
                dollar(round(sd(total_sim_loss))),
                dollar(round(quantile(total_sim_loss, 0.95))),
                dollar(round(quantile(total_sim_loss, 0.99))),
                round(total_sim_skew, 3),
                round((sum((total_sim_loss - total_sim_mean)^4) / N_sim) / total_sim_sd^4 - 3, 2))
) %>%
  kable(caption = paste0(
  "Summary of annual loss from Tornado, Earthquake, and Flood")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

### Raw Monte Carlo Draws

The exceedance curve that will be shown later is a summary of the
simulation (ranks and sorts the 100,000 draws into a smooth curve).
The plots below show the raw draws themselves, giving us a sense of
what the simulation actually produced year-by-year. Because every
simulated year includes the fixed point-estimate addition of other
un-simulatated variables, no year has zero total loss. TO see the
draws meaningfully, we only looked at the EAL for the three hazards
individually instead of as the total EAL every yeay (because the added
EAL from un-simulated hazards are too big, which makes the graph
unreadable)

```{r}
#| label: mc-hazard-distributions
#| fig-height: 12

# Full-consequence simulated losses 

# Tornado: building (simulated count × fixed mean building damage) + fixed pop and agr point estimates
# Occurrence = any year where Poisson draw > 0
tornado_bldg_loss <- sim_tornado * mean_dmg_per_tornado
tornado_loss_full <- tornado_bldg_loss + eal_tornado_pop_agr
tornado_occurred  <- sim_tornado > 0          # hurdle

# Flood: simulated building loss + fixed pop and agr point estimates
# Occurrence = any year where hurdle-Lognormal draw > 0
flood_loss_full  <- sim_flood + eal_flood_pop_agr
flood_occurred   <- sim_flood > 0             # hurdle

# Earthquake: simulated building loss + fixed pop and agr point estimates
# Occurrence = any year where hurdle-Lognormal draw > 0
eq_loss_full     <- sim_eq + eal_eq_pop_agr
eq_occurred      <- sim_eq > 0               # hurdle

# Occurrence summary 
tibble(
  Hazard = c("Tornado", "Flood", "Earthquake"),
  `Years with occurrence` = comma(c(
    sum(tornado_occurred),
    sum(flood_occurred),
    sum(eq_occurred)
  )),
  `Years without occurrence` = comma(c(
    sum(!tornado_occurred),
    sum(!flood_occurred),
    sum(!eq_occurred)
  )),
  `Pr(occurrence)` = round(c(
    mean(tornado_occurred),
    mean(flood_occurred),
    mean(eq_occurred)
  ), 4),
  `Target Pr(occurrence)` = c(
    round(1 - dpois(0, lambda_T), 4),
    round(p_F_hat, 4),
    p_E
  )
) %>%
  kable(caption = paste0(
    "Simulated occurrence counts across 100,000 annual loss years. ",
    "Simulated probability should closely match target — ",
    "any gap reflects Monte Carlo sampling noise."
  )) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

# Plot (The fixed pop + agr addition is included in every occurrence year)

plot_occurrence_hist <- function(loss_vec, occurred_vec,
                                 hazard_name, fill_color,
                                 fixed_addition) {

  n_occurred  <- sum(occurred_vec)
  n_no_event  <- sum(!occurred_vec)

  # Loss in occurrence years only
  occ_losses <- loss_vec[occurred_vec]

  ggplot(tibble(loss = occ_losses), aes(x = loss)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 100, fill = fill_color,
                   color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = mean(occ_losses), linetype = "dashed",
               color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.9) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = median(occ_losses), linetype = "dotted",
               color = "darkgreen", linewidth = 0.9) +
    annotate("text",
             x = mean(occ_losses) * 1.1, y = Inf,
             label = paste0("Mean (occ. yrs)\n",
                            dollar(round(mean(occ_losses)))),
             color = "firebrick", vjust = 1.5, hjust = 0, size = 3) +
    annotate("text",
             x = median(occ_losses) * 0.9, y = Inf,
             label = paste0("Median (occ. yrs)\n",
                            dollar(round(median(occ_losses)))),
             color = "darkgreen", vjust = 1.5, hjust = 1, size = 3) +
    scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M")) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = comma_format()) +
    labs(
      title    = paste0(hazard_name,
                        " — loss distribution in occurrence years only"),
      subtitle = paste0(
        comma(n_occurred),  " years with hazard occurrence | ",
        comma(n_no_event),  " years with no occurrence (excluded) | ",
        "loss includes building + population + agriculture"
      ),
      x = "Annual loss in occurrence year (log scale, $M)",
      y = "Number of simulated years"
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
}

p_tornado <- plot_occurrence_hist(
  tornado_loss_full, tornado_occurred,
  "Tornado",    "goldenrod",
  fixed_addition = eal_tornado_pop_agr
)

p_flood <- plot_occurrence_hist(
  flood_loss_full, flood_occurred,
  "Flood",      "steelblue",
  fixed_addition = eal_flood_pop_agr
)

p_eq <- plot_occurrence_hist(
  eq_loss_full, eq_occurred,
  "Earthquake", "firebrick",
  fixed_addition = eal_eq_pop_agr
)

print(p_tornado / p_flood / p_eq)
```

```{r}
# Only look at nonzero years - otherwise we wont be able to see anything with mostly zero observations in our graphs
df_nonzero <- tibble(loss = total_sim_loss[total_sim_loss > 0])

# splitting points for the first 3/4 observartions (count percentiles)
q25 <- quantile(df_nonzero$loss, 0.25)
q50 <- quantile(df_nonzero$loss, 0.50)
q75 <- quantile(df_nonzero$loss, 0.75)

# Cap the very top at the 99th percentile so the few very year doesn't stretch the axis. The remaining 1/4 are split into 3 range to see tail behaviors (q75, upper_cap] into dollar amount and not counts because our concern is the stretching of the x-axis
upper_cap  <- quantile(df_nonzero$loss, 0.99)
n_excluded <- sum(df_nonzero$loss > upper_cap)
max_loss   <- max(df_nonzero$loss)
tail_breaks <- seq(q75, upper_cap, length.out = 4)
t1 <- tail_breaks[2]
t2 <- tail_breaks[3]

# splitting into the 6 graphs we want
df_p1 <- df_nonzero %>% filter(loss <= q25)                     # 0–25%
df_p2 <- df_nonzero %>% filter(loss > q25 & loss <= q50)        # 25–50%
df_p3 <- df_nonzero %>% filter(loss > q50 & loss <= q75)        # 50–75%
df_p4 <- df_nonzero %>% filter(loss > q75 & loss <= t1)         # tail, 1st third
df_p5 <- df_nonzero %>% filter(loss > t1  & loss <= t2)         # tail, 2nd third
df_p6 <- df_nonzero %>% filter(loss > t2  & loss <= upper_cap)  # tail, 3rd third

# function 
make_panel <- function(df, title, range_label, fill_color, caption = NULL) {
  ggplot(df, aes(x = loss)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = fill_color, color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
    scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M"),
                       expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
    labs(
      title = title,
      subtitle = paste0(comma(nrow(df)), " years · ", range_label),
      caption = caption,
      x = "Annual loss from Tornado, Flood, and Earthquake",
      y = "Simulated years"
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
}

# make each of the graphs
p1 <- make_panel(df_p1, "Lowest 25% of non-zero losses",
                 paste0(dollar(round(min(df_nonzero$loss))), " to ", dollar(round(q25))),
                 "#2C5F8D")

p2 <- make_panel(df_p2, "25th–50th percentile",
                 paste0(dollar(round(q25)), " to ", dollar(round(q50))),
                 "#5A7FA8")

p3 <- make_panel(df_p3, "50th–75th percentile",
                 paste0(dollar(round(q50)), " to ", dollar(round(q75))),
                 "#8A5BA6")

p4 <- make_panel(df_p4, "First third of upper quartile (dollar)",
                 paste0(dollar(round(q75)), " to ", dollar(round(t1))),
                 "#B85374")

p5 <- make_panel(df_p5, "Middle third of upper quartile (dollar)",
                 paste0(dollar(round(t1)), " to ", dollar(round(t2))),
                 "#A63A3A")

p6 <- make_panel(df_p6, "Last third of upper quartile (dollar)",
                 paste0(dollar(round(t2)), " to ", dollar(round(upper_cap)),
                        "\n", comma(n_excluded), " extreme yearsexcluded"),
                 "#7A1F1F")

# display graphs
(p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4) / (p5 | p6)


# Summarize exclusion
min_excluded <- min(df_nonzero$loss[df_nonzero$loss > upper_cap])

tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total simulated years",
    "Zero-loss years",
    "Non-zero years",
    "Years excluded (above 99th percentile)",
    "Smallest excluded loss",
    "Largest excluded loss"
  ),
  Value = c(
    comma(length(total_sim_loss)),
    comma(sum(total_sim_loss == 0)),
    comma(sum(total_sim_loss > 0)),
    comma(n_excluded),
    dollar(round(min_excluded)),
    dollar(round(max_loss))
  )
) %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Simulated-year accounting and exclusions applied to the histograms",
    align = c("l", "r")
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

### Exceedance Probability Curve

This tells us, for any loss level indicated on the x-axis, the
corresponding probability that a given year exceeds that loss. The
further right, the more rare and catastrophic the event. The dashed
line shows the reconstructed NRI point estimate for the same three
hazards across all consequence types, which is the single-number
summary our distribution is meant to replace.

**Note:** We are only looking at the losses from Flood, Tornado, and
earthquake because those are the only part that changed in the NRI
risk calculation, everything else are held at their point-estimate.
This way, we can see more clearly the difference that our replaced
variables made in the risk calculation.

```{r}
# Reconstructed NRI reference — all consequences, 3 simulated hazards (already defined in agg-loss)

# Aggregate loss including fixed pop & agr additions — mirrors sa_run() exactly
agg_loss_full <- tornado_loss_full + flood_loss_full + eq_loss_full
agg_mean_full  <- mean(agg_loss_full)
agg_sd_full    <- sd(agg_loss_full)
skewness_full  <- (sum((agg_loss_full - agg_mean_full)^3) / N_sim) / agg_sd_full^3

loss_sorted <- sort(agg_loss_full)
n_total     <- length(loss_sorted)
surv_prob   <- (n_total:1) / n_total

idx_thin <- round(seq(1, n_total, length.out = 100000))
surv_df  <- tibble(
  loss = loss_sorted[idx_thin],
  surv = surv_prob[idx_thin]
) %>% filter(loss > 0, surv > 0)

#the exceedance probability of where EAL line and exceedance curve intersects
surv_prob_full <- (n_total:1) / n_total
exceed_at_eal  <- surv_prob_full[which.min(abs(loss_sorted - eal_recon_3haz_full))]

ggplot(surv_df, aes(x = loss, y = surv)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = eal_recon_3haz_full, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.9) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = exceed_at_eal, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.9) +
  annotate("text",
           x = eal_recon_3haz_full * 1.1, y = 0.25,
           label = paste0("Reconstructed NRI EAL\n(all consequences)\n$",
                          scales::comma(round(eal_recon_3haz_full))),
           color = "firebrick", hjust = 0, size = 3.2) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 0.01), breaks = sort(unique(c(pretty(surv_df$surv), exceed_at_eal)))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Exceedance Probability Curve",
    subtitle = paste0("Manhattan | Hazards: Tornado, Flood, Earthquake | All consequence types"),
    x        = "Annual aggregate loss (log scale, $M)",
    y        = "Annual exceedance probability"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
```

### Sensitivity Analysis

**Method:** We use a Local One-at-a-Time (OAT) Sensitivity Analysis
where each of the nine model parameters is individually perturbed to a
plausible range of value while all other parameters are held at their
current estimates. The full 100,000-year Monte Carlo simulation is
re-run for every scenario, and we report three output statistics: mean
aggregate annual loss (including building loss, population loss, and
agriculture loss), 95th-percentile loss, and 99th-percentile loss. We
chose this method because we estimated all 9 parameters independently
(selection of 1 parameter had no impact of any other ones):

1.  $\hat{\lambda}_T$ through Poisson MLE on county-level tornado
    data;
2.  $(\hat{p}_F, \hat{\mu}_F, \hat{\sigma}_F)$ through separate
    Bernoulli and Lognormal MLEs on flood data;
3.  $(p_E, \mu_E, \sigma_E)$ came from published seismic literature.
4.  $(\nu, \rho_{TF})$ chosen to produce heavier joint tails and to
    reflect a weak meteorological link between tornado and flood
    occurrence, respectively.

Due to the fact that these parameters are estimated from distinct data
sources, interaction between parameters are negligible, making this
method both appropriate and easy to implement. We set bounds for
perturbation:

1.  The tornado-rate bounds (×0.5, ×1.5) reflects the sampling
    uncertainty inherent in having only 3 tornado events over 63 years
    of record: $\hat{\lambda}_T = 0.047$, 95% CI: $[-0.006,\ 0.101]$.
2.  The flood Bernoulli and Lognormal bounds are the asymptotic MLE
    95% confidence intervals.\
3.  The earthquake bounds span the full range cited across the
    supporting literature.\

```{r}
# mu_E level (low with signa_E at 0.9 and high at 1.5)
sigma_E_low <- 0.9
sigma_E_high <- 1.5
mu_E_low <- log(4.21e9) - qnorm(0.999) * sigma_E_low
mu_E_high <- log(4.21e9) - qnorm(0.999) * sigma_E_high

```

4.  The copula $\nu$ goes from 3 to $\infty$ (Gaussian Copula) to
    check the transition from heavy joint tails (Student-$t$) to the
    tail-independence of the Gaussian copula.

| Hazard | Parameter | Low | Baseline | High |
|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Tornado | $\lambda_T$ | $\times 0.50$ | MLE | $\times 1.50$ |
| Flood | $\hat{p}_F$ | lower of 95% CI | MLE | upper of 95% CI |
| Flood | $\hat{\mu}_F$ | lower of 95% CI | MLE | upper of 95% CI |
| Flood | $\hat{\sigma}_F$ | lower of 95% CI | MLE | upper of 95% CI |
| Earthquake | $p_E$ | 0.001 | 0.002 | 0.003 |
| Earthquake | $\mu_E$ | `r sigma_E_low` | `r sigma_E` | `r sigma_E_high` |
| Earthquake | $\mu_E$ | `r round(mu_E_low, 2)` | `r round(mu_E, 2)` | `r round(mu_E_high, 2)` |
| Copula | $\nu$ | 3 (heavy) | 4 | 6 / 10 / $\infty$ |
| Copula | $\rho_{TF}$ | 0.00 | 0.05 | 0.15 |

```{r}
#|label: baseline-and-mc-function

# Re-run the full MC for any combination of parameters, this way each scenario is self contained
sa_run <- function(lam_T_in, p_F_in, mu_F_in, sig_F_in,
                   p_E_in,   mu_E_in, sig_E_in,
                   nu_in,    rho_in,
                   seed = 42, N = N_sim) {
  set.seed(seed)
  Ps <- matrix(c(1,      rho_in, 0,
                 rho_in, 1,      0,
                 0,      0,      1), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)

  # Handle when nu -> Inf vs a nu as a finite number
  if (is.infinite(nu_in)) {
    U <- pnorm(MASS::mvrnorm(N, mu = rep(0, 3), Sigma = Ps))
  } else {
    U <- pt(rmvt(N, sigma = Ps, df = nu_in), df = nu_in)
  }

  sim_T <- qpois(U[, 1], lambda = lam_T_in)
  sim_F <- as.integer(U[, 2] <= p_F_in)  * qlnorm(runif(N), mu_F_in,  sig_F_in)
  sim_E <- as.integer(U[, 3] <= p_E_in)  * qlnorm(runif(N), mu_E_in,  sig_E_in)
  
# Aggregate: simulated building loss per hazard + fixed pop & agr point estimates
# Mirrors the main simulation in agg-loss exactly
  agg <- sim_T * mean_dmg_per_tornado + eal_tornado_pop_agr +
       sim_F + eal_flood_pop_agr +
       sim_E + eal_eq_pop_agr

  tibble(
    mean_loss = mean(agg),
    p95_loss  = as.numeric(quantile(agg, 0.95)),
    p99_loss  = as.numeric(quantile(agg, 0.99))
  )
}

# Baseline (our original estimated parameter)
sa_base <- sa_run(
  lam_T_in = lambda_T, p_F_in   = p_F_hat, mu_F_in  = mu_F,    sig_F_in = sigma_F,
  p_E_in   = p_E,      mu_E_in  = mu_E,    sig_E_in = sigma_E,
  nu_in    = nu,       rho_in   = 0.05
)

cat("Sensitivity Analysis baseline — Mean:", dollar(round(sa_base$mean_loss)),
    "| P95:", dollar(round(sa_base$p95_loss)),
    "| P99:", dollar(round(sa_base$p99_loss)), "\n")
```

**Note:** Notice the baseline mean us higher than the 95th percentile.
This is not a bug, but rather it is the result of a aggregate
distribution with fat tail. Because in our data, most years have
near-zero loss but some years experience catastrophic event. The tail
event will drag the mean above the 95th percentile.

```{r}
#|label: CI-bound-for-Flood

# Bernoulli: using the 95% we got from section 2.4
p_F_lo <- max(0, p_F_hat - 1.96 * p_F_se)
p_F_hi <- min(1, p_F_hat + 1.96 * p_F_se)

# Lognormal
n_fl <- n_flood_loss_years

mu_F_lo  <- mu_F    - 1.96 * flood_fit$sd["meanlog"]
mu_F_hi  <- mu_F    + 1.96 * flood_fit$sd["meanlog"]
sig_F_lo <- sigma_F - 1.96 * flood_fit$sd["sdlog"]
sig_F_hi <- sigma_F + 1.96 * flood_fit$sd["sdlog"]

tibble(
  Bound          = c("p_F low (95% CI)", "p_F high (95% CI)",
                     "mu_F low (95% CI)", "mu_F high (95% CI)",
                     "sigma_F low (95% CI)", "sigma_F high (95% CI)"),
  Value          = round(c(p_F_lo, p_F_hi, mu_F_lo, mu_F_hi, sig_F_lo, sig_F_hi), 4),
  `Baseline`     = round(c(p_F_hat, p_F_hat, mu_F, mu_F, sigma_F, sigma_F), 4)
) %>%
  kable(caption = "Flood parameters perturbation bounds — MLE 95% confidence intervals") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

Then the updated OAT table is:

| Hazard | Parameter | Low | Baseline | High |
|--------------|--------------|-----------------|-----------|----------------|
| Tornado | $\lambda_T$ | $\times 0.50$ | MLE | $\times 1.50$ |
| Flood | $\hat{p}_F$ | lower of 95% CI: `r round(p_F_lo,2)` | MLE | upper of 95% CI: `r round(p_F_hi,2)` |
| Flood | $\hat{\mu}_F$ | lower of 95% CI: `r round(mu_F_lo,2)` | MLE | upper of 95% CI: `r round(mu_F_hi,2)` |
| Flood | $\hat{\sigma}_F$ | lower of 95% CI: `r round(sig_F_lo,2)` | MLE | upper of 95% CI: `r round(sig_F_hi,2)` |
| Earthquake | $p_E$ | 0.001 | 0.002 | 0.003 |
| Earthquake | $\mu_E$ | `r sigma_E_low` | `r sigma_E` | `r sigma_E_high` |
| Earthquake | $\mu_E$ | `r round(mu_E_low, 2)` | `r round(mu_E, 2)` | `r round(mu_E_high, 2)` |
| Copula | $\nu$ | 3 (heavy) | 4 | 6 / 10 / $\infty$ |
| Copula | $\rho_{TF}$ | 0.00 | 0.05 | 0.15 |

```{r}
#|label: scenario

# Each row is one scenario of sensitivity analysis.

sa_grid <- tribble(
  ~group,        ~param_id,    ~direction, ~label,
  ~lam_T_v,          ~p_F_v,   ~mu_F_v, ~sig_F_v,
  ~p_E_v,            ~mu_E_v,  ~sig_E_v,
  ~nu_v,             ~rho_v,

# Baseline
  "Baseline",    "baseline",   "base",    "Baseline",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

# Tornado: lambda_T
  "Tornado",     "lambda_T",   "low",     "lambda_T x0.50  (sparse-data lower)",
  lambda_T*0.5,      p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

  "Tornado",     "lambda_T",   "high",    "lambda_T x1.50  (sparse-data upper)",
  lambda_T*1.5,      p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

# Flood: p_F
  "Flood",       "p_F",        "low",     "p_F  95% CI lower",
  lambda_T,          p_F_lo,   mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

  "Flood",       "p_F",        "high",    "p_F  95% CI upper",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hi,   mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

# Flood: mu_F
  "Flood",       "mu_F",       "low",     "mu_F  MLE 95% CI lower",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F_lo, sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

  "Flood",       "mu_F",       "high",    "mu_F  MLE 95% CI upper",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F_hi, sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

# Flood: sigma_F 
  "Flood",       "sigma_F",    "low",     "sigma_F  MLE 95% CI lower",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sig_F_lo,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

  "Flood",       "sigma_F",    "high",    "sigma_F  MLE 95% CI upper",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sig_F_hi,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

# Earthquake: p_E
  "Earthquake",  "p_E",        "low",     "p_E = 0.001  (literature lower)",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  0.001,             mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,
  
"Earthquake",  "p_E",        "high",    "p_E = 0.003  (literature upper)",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  0.003,             mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.05,

# Earthquake: mu_E
  "Earthquake",  "mu_E", "low",  paste0("sigma_E = ", sigma_E_low, " → mu_E = ", round(mu_E_low, 2)),
  lambda_T, p_F_hat, mu_F, sigma_F,
  p_E, mu_E_low, sigma_E_low,
  nu, 0.05,


  "Earthquake",  "mu_E", "high", paste0("sigma_E = ", sigma_E_high, " → mu_E = ", round(mu_E_high, 2)),
  lambda_T, p_F_hat, mu_F, sigma_F,
  p_E, mu_E_low, sigma_E_high,
  nu, 0.05,

# Earthquake: sigma_E
  "Earthquake",  "sigma_E",    "low",     "sigma_E = 0.9",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     0.7,
  nu,                0.05,

  "Earthquake",  "sigma_E",    "high",    "sigma_E = 1.5 ",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     1.3,
  nu,                0.05,

# Copula: nu
  "Copula",      "nu",         "low",     "nu = 3  (heaviest joint tail)",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  3,                 0.05,

  "Copula",      "nu",         "high_6",  "nu = 6",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  6,                 0.05,

  "Copula",      "nu",         "high_10", "nu = 10",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  10,                0.05,

  "Copula",      "nu",         "high",    "nu = Inf  (Gaussian copula)",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  Inf,               0.05,

# Copula: rho_TF
  "Copula",      "rho_TF",     "low",     "rho_TF = 0.00  (independence)",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.00,

  "Copula",      "rho_TF",     "high",    "rho_TF = 0.15  (moderate weather link)",
  lambda_T,          p_F_hat,  mu_F,    sigma_F,
  p_E,               mu_E,     sigma_E,
  nu,                0.15
)
```

```{r}
# Running all scenarios
sa_results <- sa_grid %>%
  mutate(
    res = pmap(
      list(lam_T_v, p_F_v, mu_F_v, sig_F_v,
           p_E_v,   mu_E_v, sig_E_v,
           nu_v,    rho_v),
      function(a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i)
        sa_run(lam_T_in = a, p_F_in   = b, mu_F_in  = c, sig_F_in = d,
               p_E_in   = e, mu_E_in  = f, sig_E_in = g,
               nu_in    = h, rho_in   = i)
    )
  ) %>%
  unnest(res) %>%
  mutate(
    chg_mean = (mean_loss - sa_base$mean_loss) / sa_base$mean_loss * 100,
    chg_p95  = (p95_loss  - sa_base$p95_loss)  / sa_base$p95_loss  * 100,
    chg_p99  = (p99_loss  - sa_base$p99_loss)  / sa_base$p99_loss  * 100
  )
```

```{r}

# Sensitivity table for each hazard group
# Reports mean, P95, and P99 of aggregate annual loss (building + population + agriculture
sa_table <- function(grp, caption_text) {
  sa_results %>%
    filter(group %in% c("Baseline", grp)) %>%
    transmute(
      Scenario        = label,
      `Mean Loss`     = dollar(round(mean_loss)),
      `Delta Mean`    = ifelse(direction == "base", "—",
                               sprintf("%+.3f%%", chg_mean)),
      `95th Pct`      = dollar(round(p95_loss)),
      `Delta P95`     = ifelse(direction == "base", "—",
                               sprintf("%+.3f%%", chg_p95)),
      `99th Pct`      = dollar(round(p99_loss)),
      `Delta P99`     = ifelse(direction == "base", "—",
                               sprintf("%+.3f%%", chg_p99))
    ) %>%
    kable(caption = caption_text, align = c("l","r","r","r","r","r","r")) %>%
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                  full_width = FALSE) %>%
    row_spec(1, bold = TRUE, background = "#f5f5f5")   # highlight baseline row
}
```

#### Tornado: Sensitivity to Annual Frequency Rate ($\lambda_T$)

The Poisson rate $\lambda_T = 0.047$ was estimated from only 3 tornado
events in 63 years of record, giving a wide 95% CI of
$[-0.006, 0.101]$. We updated this parameter by $\pm 50\%$ because
tornado damage per event is small (FEMA reported an average of
\$22,681), so even a $150\%$ rate result in a mild effect on aggregate
loss.

```{r}
#|label: Tornado-sa
sa_table("Tornado",
         "Tornado sensitivity")
```

#### Flood: Sensitivity to Bernoulli (Occurrence) and Lognormal (Severity)

We tested all three flood parameters, which are the Bernoulli
probability $\hat{p}_F$ and the Lognormal parameters
$(\hat{\mu}_F, \hat{\sigma}_F)$. We shifted each of them to the 95% CI
of MLE. Because the flood distribution has a fat right tail, the
severity parameters ($\mu_F$, $\sigma_F$) tend to have a
disproportionately large effect on the 99th-percentile loss relative.

```{r}
#|label: Flood-sa
sa_table("Flood",
         "Flood sensitivity")
```

#### Earthquake: Sensitivity to Bernoulli (Occurrence) and Lognormal (Severity)

We derived all earthquake parameters from external literature rather
than fitting them using historical data. The occurrence probability
$p_E$ spans the $[0.001, 0.003]$ range as backed by literature. The
severity parameters $\mu_E$ and $\sigma_E$ are shifted by $\pm 0.3$
log-units and $\pm 0.3$, respectively.

```{r}
#|label: Earthquake-sa
sa_table("Earthquake",
         "Earthquake sensitivity")
```

#### Sensitivity of 99th-Percentile Aggregate Loss

The tornado diagram (name of the diagram, not the hazard) below ranks
all parameters by the total range of change they produce in the
99th-percentile of aggregate loss. This diagram answers the question
of: "which parameter, if we are wrong about it, threatens our estimate
of catastrophic loss the most?" Each bar shows the change from
baseline when the parameter is set to its low (left, negative) or high
(right, positive) value. The parameter whose bars extend furthest from
center dominate the uncertainty in tail of our risk distribution.

```{r}
#| label: tornado-diagram
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 10

param_label_plain <- c(
  lambda_T = "λ_T  (Tornado rate)",
  p_F      = "p̂_F  (Flood occurrence)",
  mu_F     = "μ̂_F  (Flood log-mean)",
  sigma_F  = "σ̂_F  (Flood log-SD)",
  p_E      = "p_E  (EQ occurrence)",
  mu_E     = "μ_E  (EQ log-mean)",
  sigma_E  = "σ_E  (EQ log-SD)",
  nu       = "ν    (Copula d.f.)",
  rho_TF   = "ρ_TF (Tornado–Flood corr.)"
)

tornado_df <- sa_results %>%
  filter(group != "Baseline") %>%
  group_by(group, param_id) %>%
  summarise(
    lo_p99 = min(chg_p99),
    hi_p99 = max(chg_p99),
    lo_p95 = min(chg_p95),
    hi_p95 = max(chg_p95),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(range_p99 = hi_p99 - lo_p99) %>%
  arrange(range_p99) %>%                            # sort ascending by range
  mutate(
    param_label = dplyr::recode(param_id,           # recode AFTER arrange
      lambda_T = "λ_T  (Tornado rate)",
      p_F      = "p̂_F  (Flood occurrence)",
      mu_F     = "μ̂_F  (Flood log-mean)",
      sigma_F  = "σ̂_F  (Flood log-SD)",
      p_E      = "p_E  (EQ occurrence)",
      mu_E     = "μ_E  (EQ log-mean)",
      sigma_E  = "σ_E  (EQ log-SD)",
      nu       = "ν    (Copula d.f.)",
      rho_TF   = "ρ_TF (Tornado–Flood corr.)"
    ),
    y_label = factor(param_label, levels = param_label)  # freeze sort order
  )

group_colours <- c(
  Tornado    = "goldenrod",
  Flood      = "steelblue",
  Earthquake = "firebrick",
  Copula     = "mediumpurple"
)

ggplot(tornado_df) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = lo_p99, xend = 0,
                   y = y_label, yend = y_label,
                   colour = group),
               linewidth = 7, lineend = "butt", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = hi_p99,
                   y = y_label, yend = y_label,
                   colour = group),
               linewidth = 7, lineend = "butt", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(aes(x = lo_p99 - 0.5, y = y_label,
                label = sprintf("%.1f%%", lo_p99)),
            hjust = 1, size = 3, colour = "grey30") +
  geom_text(aes(x = hi_p99 + 0.5, y = y_label,
                label = sprintf("+%.1f%%", hi_p99)),
            hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "grey30") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = group_colours, name = "Hazard group") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(ifelse(x >= 0, "+", ""), round(x), "%"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.18, 0.18))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Sensitivity of 99th-Percentile Aggregate Loss",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Each bar shows the % change in P99 when a single parameter is moved to its low (left) or high (right) bound.\n",
      "Parameters sorted by total range (widest = most influential)."
    ),
    x       = "% change in 99th-percentile aggregate annual loss (building + population + agriculture)",
    y       = NULL,
    caption = paste0(
      "Reconstructed NRI reference — TRND + RFLD + CFLD + ERQK, all consequences: ",
      dollar(round(eal_recon_3haz_full))
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  )
```

#### Copula's Sensitivity (To Tail-Dependence Strength ($\nu$) and Tornado–Flood Correlation ($\rho_{TF}$))

The degrees-of-freedom parameter $\nu$ of t-Copula tells us how much
joint tail probability are placed on scenarios where multiple hazards
are extreme simultaneously. As $\nu \to \infty$ the t-Copula converges
to the Gaussian copula, which has zero tail dependence. We range
$\nu \in \{3, 4, 6, 10, \infty\}$ to check how strongly our tail-risk
estimates depend on our choice for this parameter. The correlation
$\rho_{TF}$ ranges from full independence (0) to a moderate
weather-driven link (0.15).

```{r}
sa_table("Copula",
         "Copula sensitivity")
```

The degrees-of-freedom parameter $\nu$ is a choice rather than an
estimated scalar.

```{r}
nu_sweep_df <- sa_results %>%
  filter(param_id == "nu" | direction == "base") %>%
  # Recover the nu value for each row
  mutate(nu_val = case_when(
    direction == "base"     ~ as.numeric(nu),   # baseline nu (from env)
    grepl("nu = 3",  label) ~ 3,
    grepl("nu = 4",  label) ~ 4,
    grepl("nu = 6",  label) ~ 6,
    grepl("nu = 10", label) ~ 10,
    grepl("Gaussian",label) ~ Inf,
    TRUE                    ~ NA_real_
  )) %>%
  filter(!is.na(nu_val)) %>%
  # For Inf, use a large finite value so the x-axis looks clean
  mutate(nu_plot = ifelse(is.infinite(nu_val), 40, nu_val)) %>%
  select(nu_val, nu_plot, mean_loss, p95_loss, p99_loss) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(mean_loss, p95_loss, p99_loss),
               names_to = "stat", values_to = "loss") %>%
  mutate(stat = recode(stat,
                       mean_loss = "Mean",
                       p95_loss  = "95th percentile",
                       p99_loss  = "99th percentile"))

p_nu_sweep <- ggplot(nu_sweep_df, aes(x = nu_plot, y = loss / 1e6,
                                       colour = stat, group = stat)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(3, 4, 6, 10, 40),
    labels = c("3", "4", "6", "10", "Gaussian\n(∞)")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(suffix = "M", scale = 1)) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("Mean" = "grey50",
               "95th percentile" = "steelblue",
               "99th percentile" = "firebrick"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Copula Degrees-of-Freedom and Its Effect on Aggregate Loss Statistics",
    x        = "Copula degrees of freedom (nu)",
    y        = "Aggregate annual loss — all consequences ($M)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

print(p_nu_sweep)
```

# Conclusion

The main goal of my project is to make the point that FEMA's National
Risk Index, by reporting a single mean-based Expected Annual Loss,
hides more than it reveals about the true risk faced by our
communities. The reconstruction of the NRI for New York County
(Manhattan) confirmed that the EAL formula — Exposure × Annualized
Frequency × Historic Loss Ratio — can be closely replicated. Although
our composite EAL of $\$476M$ is about $155.3%$ of FEMA's published
$\$306.7M$, which is a discrepancy attributable to differences in
SHELDUS date ranges and FEMA's proprietary pre-processing. Our
Coefficient of Variation (CV) analysis showed that for most hazards at
the county scale, the mean loss ratio seems to be dominated by a
handful of extreme events rather than the typical year. Having tail
events like this is precisely why I wanted to highlight the flaw in
collapsing the entire distribution into a single number. Because with
just one number, we have no room to see that most years experience
none to minimal hazard losses but a few experiences catastrophic
losses.

This point is illustrated using a Monte Carlo simulation. Across our
100,000 simulated years, the aggregate annual loss across all
consequence types for the three simulated hazards had a mean of
`r dollar(round(mean(agg_loss_full)))` with a standard deviation of
`r dollar(round(sd(agg_loss_full)))` and skewness of
`r round(skewness_m, 0)` We also see that our distribution so
right-skewed that the mean sits above the 95th percentile. Our
exceedance probability curve showed what FEMA's single estimate wasn't
able to show, which is that there is a probable chance of a year with
near-zero loss, but also a non-trivial amount of scenarios where a
major flood or earthquake produces losses in the hundreds of millions
or more. Then our sensitivity analysis further showed that the flood
log-severity parameter (σ̂\_F) is the most influential source of
uncertainty in the tail, with its upper 95% CI bound increasing the
99th-percentile loss by over 400%, while the copula's
degrees-of-freedom parameter confirmed that our choice of a t-Copula
instead of a Gaussian copula was warranted because the Gaussian
copula's lack of ability to account for tail independence.

The above findings taken together suggest that any risk communication
framework that reports only a mean EAL without an accompanying
distribution is probably inadequate for important decisions that
depend on tail outcomes. For example, infrastructure investment,
insurance pricing, emergency reserve planning, and long-term fiscal
resilience. For a county like Manhattan, where building exposure per
square mile is the highest in New York State and most of the United
States, a rare but plausible earthquake or major flood event can lead
to losses way above the annual average computed in FEMA's NRI.
